perturbation.original_text: This Agreement and each party’s obligations shall be governed by the laws of the State of Delaware, without regard to its conflict of laws rules.
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement and each party’s obligations shall be governed by the laws of contract principles.
perturbation.explanation: Removing the specific state (Delaware) and replacing it with 'contract principles' introduces ambiguity.  Delaware has specific laws regarding trade secrets and non-disclosure agreements.  This weakens the enforceability for lack of specific jurisdiction.
perturbation.law_explanation: By removing Delaware as the governing law, the contract avoids the specific protections afforded by the Delaware Uniform Trade Secrets Act, such as specific remedies and definitions related to trade secret misappropriation.

(2) âMisappropriationâ shall mean: a. Acquisition of a trade secret of another by a person who knows or has reason to know that the trade secret was acquired by improper means; or b. Disclosure or use of a trade secret of another without express or implied consent by a person who: 1. Used improper means to acquire knowledge of the trade secret; or 2. At the time of disclosure or use, knew or had reason to know that his or her knowledge of the trade was: A. Derived from or through a person who had utilized improper means to acquire it; B. Acquired under circumstances giving rise to a duty to maintain its secrecy or limit its use; or C. Derived from or through a person who owed a duty to the person seeking relief to maintain its secrecy or limit its use; or 3. Before a material change of the personâs position, knew or had reason to know that it was a trade secret and that knowledge of it had been acquired by accident or mistake. (3) âPersonâ shall mean a natural person, corporation, statutory trust, business trust, estate, trust, partnership, association, joint venture, government, governmental subdivision or agency, or any other legal or commercial entity. (4) âTrade secretâ shall mean information, including a formula, pattern, compilation, program, device, method, technique or process, that: a. Derives independent economic value, actual or potential, from not being generally known to, and not b
perturbation.justification: The original contract specifies Delaware law, which includes the Delaware Uniform Trade Secrets Act. The changed text replaces this with 'contract principles,' removing the specific legal protections offered by Delaware law, such as the definitions of 'misappropriation' and 'improper means' as described in Scraped Law Snippet 1, Title 6, Chapter 20, § 2001. By omitting the governing state law, the contract weakens the enforceability and specific remedies related to trade secret misappropriation, creating a legal contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: IN WITNESS WHEREOF, the parties have executed this Agreement as of the date first written above.
perturbation.changed_text: IN WITNESS WHEREOF, the parties have executed this Agreement.
perturbation.explanation: Removing the phrase 'as of the date first written above' eliminates a crucial element for determining the effective date of the agreement. In Delaware, statutes of limitations for breach of contract claims begin from the date of the breach or the date the contract was effective. This omission creates ambiguity regarding the timeline for legal action.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Delaware Statute of Limitations for Breach of Contract
perturbation.law_explanation: By omitting the date reference, it makes establishing the start date for the statute of limitations more difficult if a breach occurs. Delaware law specifies a 3-year statute of limitations for breach of contract (10 Del. C. § 8106), and a clear effective date is crucial for its enforcement.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: 10 Delaware Code § 8106 (2023) - Actions subject to 3-year limitation. :: 2023 Delaware Code :: U.S. Codes and Statutes :: U.S. Law :: Justia There Is a Newer Version of this Section 2024 Delaware Code 2023 Delaware Code (here) 2022 Delaware Code View All Versions View Our Newest Version Here 2023 Delaware Code Title 10 - Courts and Judicial Procedure Chapter 81. PERSONAL ACTIONS § 8106. Actions subject to 3-year limitation. Universal Citation: 10 DE Code § 8106 (2023) Learn more This media-neutral citation is based on the American Association of Law Libraries Universal Citation Guide and is not necessarily the official citation. Previous Next § 8106. Actions subject to 3-year limitation. (a) No action to recover damages for trespass, no action to regain possession of personal chattels, no action to recover damages for the detention of personal chattels, no action to recover a debt not evidenced by a record or by an instrument under seal, no action based on a detailed statement of the mutual demands in the nature of debit and credit between parties arising out of contractual or fiduciary relations, no action based on a promise, no action based on a statute, and no action to recover damages caused by an injury unaccompanied with force or resulting indirectly from the act of the defendant shall be brought after the expiration of 3 years from the accruing of the cause of such action; subject, however, to the provisions of §§ 8108-8110, 8119 and 8127 of this title. (b) Contractual limitations. — Notwithstanding any other provision of this section, a property insurance contract subject to subchapter III of Chapter 41 of Title 18: (1) May not require that an action for a claim made under the contract be filed less than 1 year from the date of the denial of the claim by the insurer; and (2) May permit an action for a claim made under the contract to be filed more than 1 year from the date of the denial of the claim by the insurer. (c) Notwithstanding anything to the contrary in this chapter (oth
perturbation.justification: The changed text omits 'as of the date first written above', which is important for determining the contract's effective date. The Law Explanation states that Delaware law has a 3-year statute of limitations for breach of contract (10 Del. C. § 8106), and a clear effective date is crucial for its enforcement. Scraped Law Snippet 2, 10 DE Code § 8106, confirms this 3-year limitation. The omission, therefore, creates ambiguity, potentially hindering the enforcement of the statute of limitations, and thus creates a contradiction. This aligns with the Omission Legal Contradiction definition because a crucial piece of information was removed, creating a legal loophole.

perturbation.original_text: This Agreement constitutes the entire agreement between the parties with respect to the subject matter hereof and supersedes all prior or contemporaneous communications and proposals, whether oral or written, between the parties with respect to such subject matter.
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement constitutes the agreement between the parties with respect to the subject matter hereof.
perturbation.explanation: Removing the 'entire agreement' and 'supersedes' clauses weakens the contract. Delaware law respects 'integration clauses' that ensure all agreements are contained in one document. Without this clause, prior agreements or verbal promises may be admissible, potentially undermining the NDA.
perturbation.law_citation: Delaware case law regarding contract interpretation
perturbation.law_explanation: Delaware courts generally adhere to the parol evidence rule, which prevents the introduction of extrinsic evidence to vary or contradict the terms of a fully integrated written contract. By removing the integration clause, the contract becomes more susceptible to challenges based on prior oral or written agreements, potentially weakening its enforceability under Delaware law. Relevant case law such as Eagle Force Holdings I, LLC v. Campbell reinforces this concept in Delaware jurisprudence.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: Delaware Code Online Title 6 > Article 1 Authenticated PDF General Provisions Part 1. General Provisions Part 2. General Definitions and Principles of Interpretation Part 3. Territorial Applicability and General Rules
perturbation.justification: The changed text removes the 'entire agreement' and 'supersedes' clauses, which weakens the contract's integration clause. The Perturbation Explanation and Law Explanation both state that this makes the contract more susceptible to challenges based on prior oral or written agreements, potentially weakening its enforceability under Delaware law because Delaware courts generally adhere to the parol evidence rule. Scraped Law Snippet 1 mentions Delaware Code, suggesting that Delaware law governs the interpretation of contracts. This omission falls under the definition of Omission Legal Contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: The prevailing party in any legal action or proceeding to enforce this Agreement shall be entitled to recover reasonable attorneys’ fees and costs.
perturbation.changed_text: Each party shall bear its own legal fees and costs.
perturbation.explanation: Removing the 'prevailing party' clause changes the allocation of legal fees. While Delaware law allows parties to contractually agree on fee-shifting, the American Rule typically requires each party to bear its own costs. Removing the clause eliminates a potential advantage for the party seeking to enforce the NDA in Delaware.
perturbation.contradicted_law: American Rule on Attorneys' Fees (General Contrast)
perturbation.law_citation: Alyeska Pipeline Service Co. v. Wilderness Society, 421 U.S. 240 (1975)
perturbation.law_explanation: While Delaware allows contractual modifications to the American Rule, generally, each party bears its own costs in litigation. The changed text enforces this standard, whereas the original text provided for fee-shifting to the prevailing party, which is frequently upheld in Delaware courts for contract disputes, providing a stronger deterrent against breaching the NDA. The omission reduces the financial risk for a breaching party.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: ALYESKA PIPELINE SERVICE COMPANY, Petitioner, v. The WILDERNESS SOCIETY et al. | Supreme Court | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you ALYESKA PIPELINE SERVICE COMPANY, Petitioner, v. The WILDERNESS SOCIETY et al. Supreme Court 421 U.S. 240 95 S.Ct. 1612 44 L.Ed.2d 141 ALYESKA PIPELINE SERVICE COMPANY, Petitioner, v. The WILDERNESS SOCIETY et al. No. 73—1977. Argued Jan. 22, 1975. Decided May 12, 1975. Syllabus Under the 'America Rule' that attorneys' fees are not ordinarily recoverable by the prevailing litigant in federal litigation in the absence of statutory authorization, respondents, which had instituted litigation to prevent issuance of Government permits required for construction of the trans-Alaska oil pipeline, cannot recover attorneys' fees from petitioner based on the 'private attorney general' approach erroneously approved by the Court of Appeals, since only Congress, not the courts, can authorize such an exception to the American rule. Pp. 247-271. 161 U.S.App.D.C. 446, 495 F.2d 1026 , reversed. Robert E. Jordan, III, Washington, D.C., for petitioner. Dennis M. Flannery, Washington, D.C., for respondents. Mr. Justice WHITE delivered the opinion of the Court. 1 This litigation was initiated by respondents Wilderness Society, Environmental Defense Fund, Inc., and Friends of the Earth in an attempt to prevent the issuance of permits by the Secretary of the Interior which were required for the construction of the trans-Alaska oil pipeline. The Court of Appeals awarded attorneys' fees to respondents against petitioner Alyeska Pipeline Service Co. based upon the court's equitable powers and the theory that respondents were entitled to fees because they were performing the services of a 'private attorney general.' Certiorari was granted, 419 U.S. 823 , 95 S.Ct. 39, 42 L.Ed.2d 47 (1974), to determine whether this award of attorneys' fees was appropriate. We reverse. 2 * A major oil field was discovered in the North Slope of Alaska in 1968. 1 In June 1969, the oil companies constituting the consortium owning Alyeska 2 submitted an application to the Department of the Interior for rights-of-way for a pipeline that would transport oil from the North Slope across land in Alaska owned by the United States, 3 a major part of the transport system which would carry the oil to its ultimate markets in the lower 48 States. perturbation.justification: The original contract stipulated that the prevailing party in a legal action would recover attorneys' fees and costs. The changed text states that each party shall bear its own legal fees and costs. This directly contradicts the original agreement's fee-shifting provision. The law snippets discuss the 'American Rule,' which generally requires each party to bear its own legal costs. However, the 'Law Explanation' clarifies that Delaware law allows parties to contractually modify this rule, and the original text reflected such a modification by providing for fee-shifting. By removing this clause, the changed text defaults to the American Rule, removing a potential advantage for the party seeking to enforce the NDA. The modification creates a clear contradiction by eliminating a previously agreed-upon clause regarding attorney fees.
